item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I share this sentiment.

I want to add that this expands the rules of the Names and Fae lore in a way that encourages one to create more. At least for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A 100% unmitigated banger which builds on 4000 in a really fun, tense way and provides a great example of how to adapt folkloric material within a clearly Foundation framework in a novel and interesting way. Bravo!

item.title: SCP-5925: +-0
item.content:encoded: I wanted to upvote this, and was actually leaning towards it up until the second addendum. This is certainly interesting in how it adds to the Santesian mythos and 4000 lore of the wiki, but the naming aspect feels clunky and not very well thought out. I won't downvote as it certainly has its good points, but I also won't upvote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree, "blank is actually an SCP" has been done so many times it's gotten to be really lame, but this one is so creepy and weird, and has the potential to be incredibly destructive or incredibly useful with just a single word… it's good stuff.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with this sentiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While this has somewhat interesting ideas in how it handles 4000 lore and Santa Claus, I feel it fails to explore them in sufficient depth for it to be a satisfying article. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was skeptical, but that 4666 crosslink was especially… *chef's kiss* muah. Good shit.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so very glad this made it to the main list. It's a fun concept and escalates tension excellently within the small space it has. That brief moment between the cold settling in and the doctor acting under pressure adds so much. It's a clever piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good all around, and a very good example of how to write good dialogue.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: I wasn't expecting the fae aspect, and it was a welcome surprise. Thanks for the good read!

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Real glad to hear that! I realize making a character of popular culture into an SCP is hokey if not done well. I wouldn't have even attempted it if not for A. My recent delve into the many different folklore/mythological entities that make up our current understanding of Saint Nick, and B. The association with the fae that came to me. I figured making him a fae was the only solid way to do it.

So its relieving to know my attempts were not in vain!

At first I wasn't sold on it, and I had placed a downvote for a while, but after coming back to the addendum interview, I changed my mind. I don't like when widely-known figures are made into SCPs, but I'd have to say that this is the exception.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of his forms better be that Russian Santa from Rise Of The Guardians.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For anyone wondering Sinterklaas and Father Christmas (Kerstman in Dutch) are separate things. 5-6 december (5 in the Netherlands, 6 in Belgium) and 24-25 december respectively. And if you are wondering, yes, we get presents twice.

Sorry about the nitpick, but please know that this didn't hold my enjoyment back. It is really confusing after all. Please have my +1.

Also Hexick that name is already in user for another SCP.

Edit: corrected a date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I'm a bit disappointed that this wasn't titled "Saint Nick" (get it?), the premise is quite interesting, and the whole theme you have going on worked nicely, with few hitches.
+1

Edit: See Sirslash47's comment below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a lot better then I thought it was going to be looking at the general premise- the idea of 'Blank is actually anomalous', or 'blank is actually an SCP', is boring and has been done so many times.

I'm pleased that this didn't fall into that, and I'm always happy to see more from 4000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy this premise — I started to figure it out just before that telling crosslink, eliciting an "Aha!" from me, which is certainly a good sign. However, this piece is really quite rough around the edges. The sections written in blue appear to describe the desired version of the anomaly associated with its designation, but I got the sense that the anomaly's form is dependent on its own perception of its current name's connotations. Aside from the mechanics of the anomaly itself, I feel like the description and conprocs could have been more fleshed out and served as more of a hook for the article, as basically my only incentive to continue reading (prior to the addenda) was to see how it was connected to the region which shall remain unnamed. I'm also not really a fan of "access granted" being formatted as all lowercase, nor a single footnote that says "holly" as opposed to "Commonly known as holly" or something of that sort.

Clever, but unpolished. Novote.

EDIT: Will check back to see if my crit is implemented. Would be happy to upvote if so, because I really really enjoy this idea.

item.title: Happy Holidays!
item.content:encoded:
Sinterklaas, Father Christmas, Père Noël, Weihnachtsmann, Saint Nicholas of Myra, The Yule Goat, Odin, Saturn…

A pain in the ass by any other name.

I've been wanting to make a Holiday themed article for a long time now, and I hope you all enjoy it! Thank you to everyone that helped me polish this up. Special thanks to:

Grigori Karpin

RockTeethMothEyes

Cydhra

cybersqyd

DeLuChane

and apologies to all the other names I had forgotten!
